chief_complaint: I'm tired of being told this is just bad periods.
history_of_present_illness: Progressively worsening pelvic pain over 6 years. Severe dysmenorrhea since menarche at age 12, initially attributed to normal periods. Over the past 3 years pain has become debilitating — missing 2 to 3 days of work per month. Pain begins 24 to 48 hours before menses, peaks on days 1 to 2, described as knife-like and cramping in the lower pelvis with radiation to low back and inner thighs. Reports associated cyclic GI symptoms: bloating, diarrhea alternating with constipation, and painful bowel movements specifically around menses (dyschezia, days -1 through +3 of cycle). Between cycles, bowel habits are mostly normal. Deep dyspareunia for past 2 years — particularly with certain positions, worst in the week before menses. Has been trying to conceive with her husband for 14 months with no success; has not yet seen a fertility specialist. Has seen her PCP four times over the past 2 years; diagnosed variously as 'primary dysmenorrhea,' 'probable IBS,' and 'generalized anxiety,' started on an SSRI 8 months ago. Pain management has been limited to OTC NSAIDs and a brief trial of combined hormonal contraception, stopped 5 months ago because the patient is trying to conceive.
past_medical_history: Generalized anxiety disorder (PCP diagnosis, 2 years ago; on SSRI)
Irritable bowel syndrome (PCP clinical diagnosis; no GI workup performed)
social_history: Non-smoker. Occasional alcohol, no drug use. Middle school teacher. Married 3 years. Actively trying to conceive × 14 months.
family_history: Mother had 'heavy, painful periods' and underwent hysterectomy at age 40 for 'fibroids or something like that — they never really explained it.' Maternal grandmother also had early hysterectomy in her 40s. One younger sister with ongoing chronic pelvic pain; not yet formally evaluated.
physical_exam: Alert, mildly uncomfortable-appearing but not in acute distress. Abdomen soft with mild tenderness in bilateral lower quadrants; no rebound or guarding; no palpable masses. Pelvic exam: external genitalia normal. Speculum: cervix without discharge or lesions. Bimanual: uterus anteverted, mobile but tender; possible nodularity palpated in posterior cul-de-sac, patient reports sharp pain on posterior palpation. Adnexa non-tender, no discrete masses appreciated. Rectovaginal exam: tenderness posterior to cervix; patient states 'that is the pain I feel during sex.' No rectal masses.

initial_workup.transvaginal_ultrasound: Uterus 8.2 x 4.6 x 5.1 cm, anteverted, normal contour. Endometrium 7 mm, trilaminar. Both ovaries normal size and appearance, no masses or endometriomas. No free fluid in cul-de-sac. Official read: normal study.
